text: During the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the surgeon reveals the gallbladder, which is completely covered by the omentum within the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon attempts to separate the omentum from the head of the gallbladder, gradually freeing it from adhesions. The gallbladder is partially covered by inflamed, reddish omentum, with stronger adhesions near the fundus. The surgeon uses a tooth forceps to hold and push back the head of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to control bleeding while dissecting around the base of the gallbladder, where it attaches to the cystic duct. Care is taken to avoid collateral damage to nearby structures such as the colon, duodenum, and small intestine.

The surgeon uses tooth forceps to hold the gallbladder fundus and push it back, causing some omentum to fall away while other parts remain adhered. The dissection progresses towards the gallbladder triangle, with the visceral peritoneum being separated from the gallbladder. The cystic artery becomes visible, though it appears thickened and translucent rather than transparent.

The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold the gallbladder fundus while attempting to separate the gallbladder from surrounding structures. The gallbladder is closely adhered to the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and portal vein. Using a Maryland curve dissector, the surgeon carefully dissects near the gallbladder to find a cleavage plane, aiming to secure the ducts. The cystic artery is visible and being separated from the gallbladder, with some bleeding occurring during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the infundibulum of the gallbladder, aiming to isolate the cystic duct. Using a curved Maryland dissector, the surgeon applies gentle pressure to separate the thickened peritoneal tissue, revealing the space between the gallbladder and surrounding structures. The gallbladder is pulled upwards and towards the left side to facilitate the dissection, while protecting the major biliary ducts located below.

The surgeon carefully peels the gallbladder off from the underlying structures, including the major biliary ducts, portal vein, and hepatic artery, which are encased in fibrous tissue. Using gentle traction and counter-traction, a plane is developed posterior to the gallbladder. The tissue near the fundus is thicker, requiring careful dissection to delineate the cystic duct and artery. Clips are applied to the cystic duct, ensuring no damage to vital structures, with the anatomy clearly visible.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring secure placement by using two clips proximally on each structure. After clipping, the structures are cut between the clips. The monopolar cautery hook is used to address thickened tissue near the gallbladder fundus, revealing a single stone and signs of chronic inflammation. The gallbladder is submitted for histopathological examination to rule out malignancy.

The surgeon continues dissection of the gallbladder fundus, which remains slightly attached to the liver, amidst visible chronic inflammation and adhesions. Hemostasis is maintained at the gallbladder bed.
